content:
7 min read
Just now

--

I’ve watched more startups die from premature abstraction than from spaghetti code.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Lance Anderson on UnsplashA founder once showed me his codebase with visible pride. Four layers, strict dependency inversion, repository interfaces for a database that would never be swapped, use-case classes wrapping single-line operations. It was textbook Clean Architecture.
The product had eleven users. Total. And every new feature took three times longer to build than it should have, because every change touched four files instead of one.
I’m not against Clean Architecture. I’m against applying it as a default instead of a decision. This is the argument for treating architectural rigor as something you earn the need for, not something you install on day one because a book told you to.
Why This Needs Saying in 2026
AI coding tools have made it faster than ever to generate heavily-layered, “best practice” boilerplate — ask an agent for a Clean Architecture scaffold and you’ll get one in thirty seconds, interfaces and all. That’s exactly why this conversation matters more now, not less. The cost of generating over-engineering has dropped to nearly zero, but the cost of living inside it every day hasn’t changed at all.
